item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
First image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0.

Second image belongs to Michael Leese. it is not under an explicit Creative Commons license.

Both images have since been removed.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a chance to talk to Ihp about this skip in the IRC. The direction that was decided was best to head was describe potential similarities between the weapons in the game, and another weapons based skip. Given that the weapons in SCP-1438 become energy weapons upon interrogation, a neat little crosslink would be to mention similarities between it and the big energy weapon skip SCP-2820. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1438-3 are functionally and visually identical to non-anomalous "weapons" delivered at the beginning of a game of SCP-1438, unless used by an instance of SCP-1438-A or SCP-1438-B. If used on an instance of SCP-1438-A or -B, the ████ launchers (SCP-1438-3A) turn into laser-like weaponry, capable of causing 4th degree burns; socks/grenades (SCP-1438-3B) emit a wavelength of light upon detonation that disintegrates human flesh; finally, the polyethylene swords (SCP-1438-3C) become [DATA EXPUNGED]. All individuals killed in a game of SCP-1438 through use of SCP-1438-3 are treated by all persons on-campus and relations as if they had transferred to another college and cut off all contact with friends, family, etc.

To this:

SCP-1438-3 are functionally and visually identical to non-anomalous "weapons" delivered at the beginning of a game of SCP-1438, unless used by an instance of SCP-1438-A or SCP-1438-B. If used on an instance of SCP-1438-A or -B, the ████ launchers (SCP-1438-3A) turn into laser-like weaponry, capable of causing 4th degree burns;1 socks/grenades (SCP-1438-3B) emit a wavelength of light upon detonation that disintegrates human flesh; finally, the polyethylene swords (SCP-1438-3C) become [DATA EXPUNGED]. All individuals killed in a game of SCP-1438 through use of SCP-1438-3 are treated by all persons on-campus and relations as if they had transferred to another college and cut off all contact with friends, family, etc.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. It has been noted that this weaponry operates as miniature versions of the weapons systems possessed by SCP-2820. Investigation into a potential link between Mr. Finney and the developers of SCP-2820 is ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. I played in, and was a moderator for, 8 HvZ games while I was an undergraduate. This strikes a special cord of nostalgia for me.

+1 Ihp. Hope you got your chance to play.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the Mark was Rammstein's logo for a second.

I really enjoy the twist that the game doesn't take a turn for the insidious. I first read it expecting some buildup to something dark and serious, but it does the contrary to great effect. The manual excerpts (and the concern the creator has for student grades) warmed me inside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a bit underwhelmed by the end. Was expecting some hints that something more sinister going on.

Also there is effectively no containment here. This is a SCP that causes widespread, permanent effects on civilians (including people being 'unpersoned') and the foundation approach is to just watch?

Has there been any attempt to trace the website and owner or interogate delivery drivers? Or at least make all campuses ban the game under some pretense (distraction/leads to dangerous behaviour accidents/whatever)?

Or block the website? There are dozens of scp websites already censored by foundation. Unless there is something anomalous about website why can't campus firewalls just block access to it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this boring all the way through, basically amounting to a bland description of a game more fun to play than to read about. I don't know if it's a problem with myself but I could barely keep track of the goals and weapon "functions". First it says that the "grenades" cause 4th degree burns, then it says that people "killed" are basically UnPersoned by others, but are otherwise alive. So are they alive or dead? Or is this implying that people DO die during these games, and just end up being UnPersoned even in death?

That sounds like something vastly more important than is indirectly implied in the article (namely, burning bodies littering the campus with everyone around just ignoring them)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made some fixes, and the description of what I did wouldn't fit in the edit description box, so here's what I did (nothing major; don't worry).

Campuses in which SCP-1438 are taking place

As SCP-1438 refers to the concept of the game, a singular entity, "SCP-1438 are" is grammatically incorrect. I changed it to "sessions of SCP-1438 are" although "SCP-1438 is" would be just as correct if you think that works better.

One (1) Patch

Uncapitalized "patch," since I don't really see the reason it was capitalized in the first place.

but for the sake of this document, will be referred to

I'm fairly certain there shouldn't be a comma there, so I deleted it.

I changed "restroom" to "restrooms" in the game rules about safe places, to match "classrooms" in the same sentence.

as a reward for successfully coercing information from SCP-1438-3B

I laughed, because this sentence implied that players of the game are coercing information from a sock/grenade. I figure this was supposed to mean SCP-1438-B, so I changed it to that, instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good idea, and I for one will adopt it for all applicable future articles.

item.title: Finney
item.content:encoded:
One thing about Easter eggs in Foundation articles is that the Foundation would do everything they can to make details like this understandable if there's the slightest chance that they could be relevant.

For SCP-1247, the Foundation doesn't need to cite the song "Actual Cannibal Shia Labeouf", because there's no way that they could know that it's the basis for the SCP.

But here, the fact that Jack Finney wrote "Invasion of the Body Snatchers" could be very relevant. Put in a footnote - "this is presumed to be a pseudonym; Jack Finney was an American science fiction writer (1920-1995) whose work included Invasion of the Body Snatchers."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it just me or does anybody else find themselves wanting a simple index of items whenever you're reading an article dealing with more than one items. Something simple like…

The anomolous items classified in scp-1438 are designated as follows:
SCP-1438 - the game itself
SCP-1438-A - member of the group known as "The Resistance"
SCP-1438-B - member of the group known as "The Invasion"
SCP-1438-1 - website where materials to begin a game of SCP-1438 are ordered
SCP-1438-2 - a mark used to convert SCP-1438-A into SCP-1438-B
SCP-1438-3 - items converted to an alternate state during the game

I say this because sometimes it's impossible to follow all fo these designations throughout an article and I think even the author can miss things. Like the fact that there is no escription given of SCP-1438-2 in this article, except for some vague reference to it being a "tattoo" and the image on the page. But there is no actual section describing it, which is wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally got around to checking on the changes, and that's enough to push this into upvote. Well done sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this game. It's got the open-ended team tactics potential of Humans Vs Zombies, and the paranoia mind-game strategies of Assassins or Killers. I may very well try to turn this into a real thing at the next suitable occasion.

I got a little confused about where it transitioned from delusions to real world effects, particularly in the paragraph that deals with the "weapons technology". But re-reading straightened everything out, and the dawning realization that it was much more serious than my first impression added to the overall impact. So it may be something to consider revising, or it may be something you don't want to touch. This also might be an experience unique to me and so of no consequence.

One thing I'm certain of, this needs some clarification on what happens when somebody wins. That's a major mechanic of any game, and very relevant data regarding how this anomaly manifests. The absence is glaring. Victorious invaders could stay in character perpetually and attempt to instigate new games at other locations, reinforcing the whole "is it a real invasion?" vibe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP because of stuff like that the Geneva convention doesn't apply to interrogating non-humans. It reminds me of 1239. Something that looks relatively innocent at first, but does some terribly wrong stuff, very "fridge logical".

I would really appreciate a bit more of this, also the talk with the maker. No vote so far.

Also, I quite liked the expungement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last half of the description (before the documents) could use a little cleaning. Almost every line mentions some part of SCP-1438 by its designation and because of that, looking at the entry as a whole makes it look messy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The trademark is "NERF" in all capitals, along with that particular font. The brand is "Nerf", specifically Nerf Blasters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't hitting my buttons as much as it is many of the other readers. I don't know, it's written very well, certainly enough to not consider downvoting, but it's too much based around an existing game with the 'except it's real' tacked on, and not enough of the writer's imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the same. I remember playing this game when it was called assassins. So its a version of that game only its for real…I just cant get into it. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How many people have you lasered to death with a foam sword? One? Two? Fifteen? You could have nearly killed that poor kid across the hallway a few years ago, might've blasted his face off with a nerf dart. What if you'd hit that other guy with a sock? How would you live with the guilt?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same, it's a lot creepier to think that they're out there oblivious to everything that happened …or that you yourself could be a former player…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a reformed LARPer, this is great and creepy. The scariest thing is, when I was really into RP back in college, I would have loved something that was real and hardcore like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like this.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Attempts by the Foundation to perform DDoS attacks or otherwise block access to SCP-1438-1 have resulted in computers used to access the website to be permanently banned from access"
I would think the foundation would use this in their containment procedures, setting it up so that all computers on as many campuses as possible get banned from the website.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor Tweak: I've added what happens when the game ends, as per several requests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this needs a bit more detail, and I also don't like that it's just an over the top game. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. The second picture could stand to be a bit smaller, as now it's starting to get that weird pixelated thing when you squeeze a large file into a small space.

2.

the polyethylene swords (SCP-1438-3C) become [DATA EXPUNGED]

Um, the plasma swords from Halo? It also mentions what they become later in the article, that they become "Joplin's newly developed plasma swords". The data expunged could work here, but really, what unmentionable thing could it become that the Foundation has to redact it?

3. What happens if a player of the game disobeys one of the rules? How do they die? Do they just spontaneously combust?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also like the first option better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 4th Degree means the damage has reached all the way through the muscle, and the bones themselves have been burned. Very, very nasty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the first option better, personally. I think it keeps the article away from "game thing what makes you crazy" territory, you know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Truthfully… I couldn't decided on what happened when the game ended, so I kind of just omitted that and tried to leave it ambiguous.

I had two options: either they all space out after the end of the game and go back to their everyday lives, thinking "Well, that was fun. We'll have to do that again sometime", or they retain their personalities from the game and attempt to participate in future games of Invaders on campus (if Human) or attempt to assimilate to human lifestyle, blend in, and then disperse so they can affect other games on other campuses (if Invaders).

e: coin flip says numero dos, and I fail at logic for-evv-ur!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs a bit more detail, methinks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was about to say the same thing; I was wondering how something like this could end? It seems like either the invaders all get tortured to death somehow, or everyone gets Marked. Then what? I guess if they're all dead, everyone just assumes they moved away, but what about the people left? Do they remember killing them, or the war with the Invaders? If everyone is Marked, are their personalities overwritten? What happens then?

Sorry for the wall of text; I actually enjoyed this and upvoted eagerly, but I really wonder what happens when one of these games is over. There doesn't seem to be any contingency plan in the containment procedures either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, there are six.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are 4th degree burns even a thing? I thought 3rd degree burns were as high as it went.

Also, I agree with Lumancer that what happens when the game ends needs to be included.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I prefer Assassin's Guild in terms of silly university games, so I'm going to have to downvote.

Well, I'm actually downvoting because its just humans vs. zombies but with real weapons and you don't go anywhere interesting with it, which isn't really inventive enough for me to get behind.

However, I am intrigued as to how well Sal would do in this game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this, but the fact that we are not told what happens when the game ends is too huge an oversight to ignore…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember playing this scip Freshman year. It was fun.

However, this is stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any SCP that can realistically include nerf guns gets my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While this could be described as OTT humans vs zombies, it is still fantastic, I still loved reading it, and you're still getting my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you mean the Body Snatchers reference? I thought it's too obvious to be pointed out here.

Anyway, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno.

I mean, you didn't write this at all poorly, so there's that.

On the other hand, this is literally just Humans vs Zombies, the campus game that I've watched people play for like eight semesters now, but done OTT.

If I wasn't already aware of the game in reality, I probably would have upvoted this, but as I am aware… I dunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm somewhat surprised that nobody reading this has caught the little Easter Egg I hid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless it's copyrighted in some way that I'm not aware of, you still can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …god fuck, I completely forgot about that song. If I remembered it, I would've made the Mark that cool ant symbol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WE ARE THE PRODIGY.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
INVADERS

MUST DIE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I actually feel the "DEATH FOR REALS" part is unnecessary and feels rather cliche. The game simply forcing an alternate reality on players and robbing them of agency is scary enough, especially with an implication that this is the goal of the real Invaders…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wikipedia says it's the other way around…

Nerf (trademarked in capitals as NERF)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nerf (lowercase) is a brand. Should probably either capitalize it properly and remove the quotes, or say "####-brand foam dart blaster" or something to that degree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Totally flashes me back to Killer games I played in college and at conventions. Many hours of fun trying not to get arrested and shooting friends with bananas.

I'm unclear about one aspect of the rules. The Invaders goal is to infect all of humanity. Is this limited only to other players, or do they pull in outsiders with the mark? What happens when the game ends with the invaders winning? Are the players personalities altered? Perhaps also some mention of the necessary PR spin needed after "advanced technology" has been used? I would think the media would be all over it if "kids are killing each other."

Upvoted with nostalgia

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought you might have taken some inspiration from Humans Vs. Zombies! Of course, there are a lot of campus games out there that follow the "humans vs. X" format so I wasn't sure if this was already a thing (upon retrospect, if it was already a thing you'd probably have mentioned that in the article. Whoops!)

Anyway, that's awesome. This is a game I really want to try out myself.

…except, you know, I don't wanna die.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed all the issues we discussed in chat.
Earns a well-deserved upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Half and Half! It's based off of the real game Humans Vs Zombies, which I hope to play someday. However, I made up the whole alien angle, and the symbols and such to make it more subtle than HvZ (you wear headbands to designate your zombiness in that game) and try to add a sense of paranoia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
YES.

I really like this one, the game itself sounds awesome and the anomaly just adds to it.

Did you make this game up, or did you learn about it somewhere?

Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp. It's up.

Thanks to everyone who took a look at this.
